topics.title: The Origin of SRE at Google

hook.text: Google needed a new way to run systems. Ben Treynor was hired to build an ops team using code.

buildup.text: Manual ops couldn't keep pace with Google's explosive growth. Servers multiplied by thousands.

discovery.text: Treynor applied software engineering to operations. Automate the repetitive. Measure everything.

twist.text: SRE isn't ops renamed. It's a discipline with its own practices, metrics, and principles.

climax.text: Google published the SRE book in 2016. It became the industry bible for reliability.

punchline.text: SRE: engineering applied to operations problems.

quiz.question: What approach did SRE bring to operations?
options: Software engineering principles applied to operations
Hiring more operations staff
Outsourcing all server management

hook.text: DevOps is a philosophy. SRE is an implementation. Google calls SRE a class implementing DevOps.

buildup.text: DevOps says break silos and automate. It's a cultural movement, not a prescriptive framework.

discovery.text: SRE prescribes how: define SLOs, use error budgets, cap toil at 50%, run blameless postmortems.

twist.text: Small teams can adopt SRE practices without a formal SRE title. The principles scale down.

climax.text: DevOps and SRE share values: automation, measurement, collaboration. They complement each other.

punchline.text: DevOps is the why. SRE is one way to do it.

quiz.question: How does SRE relate to DevOps?
options: SRE is a prescriptive implementation of DevOps principles
SRE replaces DevOps entirely
They are completely unrelated disciplines

hook.text: An SRE must pass the same coding bar as a software engineer. Then add systems knowledge on top.

buildup.text: SREs need software engineering, networking, OS internals, and distributed systems knowledge.

discovery.text: Google requires SREs to spend at least 50% of time on engineering, not manual operations.

twist.text: SREs aren't firefighters. They design systems that prevent fires in the first place.

climax.text: The best SREs understand the full stack, from app code to kernel behavior.

punchline.text: Code skills plus systems thinking. That's an SRE.

quiz.question: What distinguishes an SRE from a traditional operations role?
options: SREs must have software engineering skills and spend time coding
SREs only handle server hardware
SREs work exclusively on networking

hook.text: Chasing 100% uptime is pointless. Users can't tell 99.99% from 99.999%, but the cost is huge.

buildup.text: Each extra nine of availability costs ten times more in engineering effort.

discovery.text: SRE says: figure out how much reliability users need. Invest exactly that much.

twist.text: A phone drops 1% of connections. Your extra 0.001% of uptime is invisible behind it.

climax.text: Embracing risk means accepting some failures. The error budget defines how much.

punchline.text: Don't chase perfection. Chase the right level.

quiz.question: Why does SRE embrace risk rather than targeting 100% uptime?
options: Each extra nine of reliability costs disproportionately more
Because failures don't matter to users
To save on hiring costs

hook.text: Not every service gets an SRE team. Google's SREs choose which services to support.

buildup.text: Services must prove readiness: monitoring, docs, SLOs. Only then does an SRE team engage.

discovery.text: SRE teams can hand back a service that becomes too unreliable. Dev teams must fix it.

twist.text: Consulting SREs advise teams without taking on-call duty. Support without full engagement.

climax.text: The model creates healthy incentives. Dev teams earn SRE support by building well.

punchline.text: SRE support is earned. Reliability is the entry ticket.

quiz.question: What must a service demonstrate to earn SRE team support?
options: Readiness through monitoring, documentation, and SLOs
High revenue generation
A large development team

hook.text: The team tracked CPU usage as their top metric. Users were unhappy anyway. Wrong measurement.

buildup.text: Service level indicators measure what users experience, not what servers report.

discovery.text: For web apps: latency and availability. For pipelines: freshness and correctness.

twist.text: Measure at the boundary closest to users. Load balancer metrics beat server metrics.

climax.text: Good SLIs are precise: 'Proportion of requests returning 2xx within 300 ms.'

punchline.text: Measure what users feel. That's the right SLI.

quiz.question: What should SLIs measure?
options: What users actually experience
Internal server CPU utilization
Total number of deployed servers

hook.text: The team set 99.999% availability. They burned through the budget in a week.

buildup.text: SLO targets should be ambitious but achievable. Start with current performance.

discovery.text: Use six months of data. If you've been 99.95% available, 99.9% is a solid start.

twist.text: Too-tight SLOs paralyze teams. Too-loose SLOs allow sloppy work. Balance matters.

climax.text: Review SLOs quarterly. Tighten as the system improves. Relax if they're unrealistic.

punchline.text: Start realistic. Tighten gradually. SLOs evolve.

quiz.question: How should SLO targets be initially set?
options: Based on current performance data, then tightened over time
Always at 99.999% for every service
As loose as possible to avoid alerts

hook.text: The error budget ran out. Devs wanted to ship. SREs said no. Who wins? The policy decides.

buildup.text: An error budget policy defines responses when the budget is healthy, low, or exhausted.

discovery.text: Budget healthy: ship freely. Budget low: slow down. Budget gone: freeze and fix reliability.

twist.text: The policy removes politics. It's not SRE vs dev. It's math. A number and a rule.

climax.text: Write the policy before a crisis. Teams agree in calm and follow rules under pressure.

punchline.text: Agree on rules before the budget runs dry.

quiz.question: What happens when an error budget is exhausted?
options: Feature releases freeze until reliability improves
Nothing changes, teams keep shipping
The SRE team is disbanded

hook.text: The error budget has 30 days of runway. A bad deploy burns a day's worth every hour.

buildup.text: Burn rate measures how fast the budget is consumed. 1x is on track. 10x is a crisis.

discovery.text: Multi-window alerting checks two time windows. A fast burn in both the last hour and six hours pages.

twist.text: Brief spikes that recover don't fire alerts. Only sustained burns that threaten the SLO do.

climax.text: Google replaced hundreds of threshold alerts with a few SLO-based burn rate alerts.

punchline.text: Alert on budget burn rate, not raw thresholds.

quiz.question: What does burn rate measure in SRE?
options: How fast the error budget is being consumed
How many servers are running hot
The rate of new code deployments

hook.text: Should we migrate databases this weekend? Check the error budget. Plenty of room? Go.

buildup.text: SLOs turn 'should we risk it' into a data-driven decision. The budget speaks.

discovery.text: If the SLO is at risk, reliability work trumps features. It's objective, not political.

twist.text: Over-serving the SLO means over-investing. Ship riskier features when budget is flush.

climax.text: PMs, SREs, and devs use SLOs as a shared language. '30% budget left' is clear to all.

punchline.text: Let the error budget decide. Data over debate.

quiz.question: How do SLOs help prioritize work?
options: They provide objective data to decide between features and reliability work
They eliminate the need for project managers
They automatically deploy fixes

hook.text: Every Monday the SRE restarts a flaky service. Every Thursday they clear logs. Pure toil.

buildup.text: Toil is manual, repetitive work that scales linearly with service size.

discovery.text: Not all manual work is toil. Planning and design are valuable. Toil produces no enduring value.

twist.text: Google's rule: no more than 50% of SRE time on toil. The rest goes to engineering.

climax.text: Left unchecked, toil grows. Each new service adds more. Automation is the only escape.

punchline.text: Toil is the enemy of engineering. Automate it away.

quiz.question: What defines toil in the SRE context?
options: Manual, repetitive work that produces no lasting value
All work performed by operations teams
Writing automation scripts

hook.text: The team felt overwhelmed but couldn't prove it. Without data, no case for automation.

buildup.text: Categorize work: on-call responses, manual deploys, ticket handling, data fixes.

discovery.text: Log time spent on each category weekly. Patterns emerge. The biggest toil sources appear.

twist.text: Some toil hides as 'normal work.' Question every repeated task. Could a script do this?

climax.text: Show leadership the numbers: '60% manual. Automation could cut it to 20%.' Data wins.

punchline.text: Measure toil to justify killing it. Data wins.

quiz.question: Why is it important to measure toil?
options: To justify automation investment with concrete data
To punish slow team members
To increase the operations budget

hook.text: One afternoon of coding automated a 4-hour weekly task. That's 200 hours saved per year.

buildup.text: Start with high-frequency, low-complexity toil. Quick wins build momentum.

discovery.text: Automation has levels: assist, partial, and full. Progress through them step by step.

twist.text: Not everything deserves automation. A yearly 10-minute task isn't worth a week of coding.

climax.text: Self-healing systems detect problems, apply fixes, and log actions. Zero human touch.

punchline.text: Automate the right things. Start small, grow bold.

quiz.question: What is the best starting point for automation?
options: High-frequency, low-complexity tasks for quick wins
The most complex system in the infrastructure
Tasks that happen only once a year

hook.text: Devs filed a ticket for every new database. SREs built them by hand. A portal ended the wait.

buildup.text: Self-service lets developers provision resources and deploy without ops tickets.

discovery.text: Internal developer platforms combine self-service with guardrails and policies.

twist.text: Self-service doesn't mean no rules. Templates and quotas keep things safe.

climax.text: Backstage, Humanitec, and Kratix are popular platforms enabling self-service at scale.

punchline.text: Empower devs with self-service. Encode rules in the platform.

quiz.question: What is the benefit of self-service platforms for SRE teams?
options: They eliminate manual provisioning tickets and bottlenecks
They remove all security controls
They replace the need for developers

hook.text: Black Friday traffic hit 3x projections. Servers maxed out. The team had planned for only 2x.

buildup.text: Capacity planning forecasts future compute, storage, and bandwidth needs.

discovery.text: Combine historical trends, business forecasts, and load test results for accuracy.

twist.text: Over-provisioning wastes money. Under-provisioning causes outages. The balance is the art.

climax.text: Load testing validates plans. Simulate projected traffic and find bottlenecks early.

punchline.text: Plan for future traffic, not today's. Test before peak.

quiz.question: What is the goal of capacity planning?
options: To forecast and provision resources for future demand
To minimize the number of servers at all costs
To delay infrastructure upgrades as long as possible

hook.text: 2:14 AM. The pager fires. Error rates spike 40x. The on-call has five minutes to respond.

buildup.text: An incident is an unplanned disruption needing urgent response. Severity sets the pace.

discovery.text: Phases: detection, triage, mitigation, resolution. Each has a clear goal and owner.

twist.text: Mitigate first, investigate later. A revert can restore service in minutes.

climax.text: Every minute of a major incident costs money and trust. Speed matters enormously.

punchline.text: Detect fast. Mitigate fast. Investigate after recovery.

quiz.question: What should be the first priority during an incident?
options: Mitigate impact and restore service quickly
Find the root cause before taking action
Write a detailed postmortem document

hook.text: Twelve engineers debugged at once. Nobody led. Everyone pulled in different directions.

buildup.text: Without structure, incident response is chaos. People duplicate work or miss tasks.

discovery.text: The Incident Commander delegates and tracks progress. They don't debug themselves.

twist.text: Roles separate concerns: one leads, another debugs, another communicates updates.

climax.text: ICS comes from firefighting. FEMA uses the same structure. It scales to any size.

punchline.text: Clear roles prevent chaos. One commander, focused teams.

quiz.question: What is the Incident Commander's primary responsibility?
options: Coordinating the response and delegating tasks
Personally debugging the root cause
Communicating with external customers

hook.text: The engineer who caused the outage was fired. Next time, nobody reported issues. Fear won.

buildup.text: Blame creates hiding. If people fear punishment, bugs go underground. Mistakes repeat.

discovery.text: Blameless postmortems assume people made the best decisions with available information.

twist.text: Blameless isn't accountable-less. The system is accountable, not the individual.

climax.text: Etsy, Google, and Netflix practice blameless postmortems and share them publicly.

punchline.text: Blame the system, not the person. Learning beats fear.

quiz.question: What is the core principle of blameless postmortems?
options: Focus on systemic causes rather than blaming individuals
Never hold anyone accountable
Only blame senior engineers

hook.text: The postmortem said 'be more careful.' Three months later, the same outage happened again.

buildup.text: A good postmortem covers timeline, impact, root cause, and action items with owners.

discovery.text: Ask 'why' five times. Each answer digs deeper toward the systemic root cause.

twist.text: Actions must be specific: 'Add config validation to CI by March 15, owned by Alice.'

climax.text: Share postmortems widely. Other teams learn from your incidents without suffering them.

punchline.text: Dig deep. Assign actions. Share the lessons widely.

quiz.question: What makes a postmortem action item effective?
options: It is specific, has an owner, and a deadline
It says 'be more careful next time'
It assigns blame to the person responsible

hook.text: The DR plan said 'fail over to the backup region.' Nobody tested it. It didn't work.

buildup.text: Game days simulate real incidents in a controlled setting. Find gaps before they matter.

discovery.text: Start small: kill one service. Then a node. Then simulate a region failure.

twist.text: Game days test people and processes, not just systems. Does the runbook actually work?

climax.text: Netflix runs Chaos Monkey continuously. Random production failures keep teams sharp.

punchline.text: Practice failure before it finds you. Drills build confidence.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of game days?
options: To test incident response in a controlled environment
To give the team a day off
To deploy new features faster

hook.text: One database handled everything. It died at 3 AM. The entire platform went dark.

buildup.text: A single point of failure means one component can take down the whole system.

discovery.text: Active-passive: a backup waits. Active-active: both serve traffic. Speed vs complexity.

twist.text: Redundancy isn't free. Running two of everything doubles the cost. Choose wisely.

climax.text: Test failover regularly. Untested failover is theoretical. Assume it won't work.

punchline.text: No single points of failure. Test the backup path.

quiz.question: What is the difference between active-passive and active-active redundancy?
options: Active-passive has a standby backup; active-active has both handling traffic
They are the same concept with different names
Active-passive is always more expensive

hook.text: Service A calls B. B is down. A retries a thousand times per second, drowning both.

buildup.text: Without protection, one failing service drags down its callers in a cascading crash.

discovery.text: A circuit breaker stops calls to a broken service. Closed, open, and half-open states.

twist.text: When open, return a fallback: cached data or a graceful error. Degraded beats dead.

climax.text: Netflix's Hystrix popularized the pattern. Today Istio and Resilience4j provide it.

punchline.text: Stop calling the broken thing. Fail fast, recover smart.

quiz.question: What does a circuit breaker do when it detects a failing service?
options: It stops sending requests and returns a fallback response
It retries faster to overcome the failure
It shuts down the calling service

hook.text: The recommendation engine crashed. The site showed 'most popular' instead. Nobody noticed.

buildup.text: Graceful degradation keeps core features running when secondary components fail.

discovery.text: Rank features by criticality. Checkout must work. Recommendations can wait.

twist.text: Design fallbacks before you need them. During an outage, it's too late to improvise.

climax.text: Feature flags enable instant degradation. Flip a flag to disable the broken feature.

punchline.text: Serve less rather than nothing. Slow beats broken.

quiz.question: What is graceful degradation?
options: Keeping core features running while disabling non-critical ones during failure
Shutting down the entire system during any failure
Ignoring all errors and hoping they resolve

hook.text: A viral tweet sent 100x normal traffic. Without limits, servers crashed for everyone.

buildup.text: Load shedding drops excess requests so the system can serve the majority of users.

discovery.text: Rate limiting caps requests per client. Excess calls get a 429 Too Many Requests reply.

twist.text: Priority shedding is smarter. Drop background tasks first. Keep user requests flowing.

climax.text: Token bucket and leaky bucket smooth bursty traffic into steady, manageable flows.

punchline.text: When traffic surges, shed load. Protect the many from the surge.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of load shedding?
options: To drop excess traffic and protect the system for remaining users
To increase server capacity automatically
To redirect all traffic to a backup data center

hook.text: You think the system survives a database crash. Have you actually killed it to prove it?

buildup.text: Chaos engineering runs controlled experiments by injecting failures into production.

discovery.text: Form a hypothesis. Run the experiment. Observe. Conclude. It's the scientific method.

twist.text: Start small: kill one pod. Grow to a node. Then a zone. Expand as confidence grows.

climax.text: Chaos engineering finds weaknesses that unit tests can't. Real traffic, real dependencies.

punchline.text: Break things on purpose. Surprise failures are worse.

quiz.question: What is the core idea behind chaos engineering?
options: Deliberately inject failures to discover system weaknesses
Randomly delete production data
Avoid testing in production at all costs

hook.text: The SRE team spent 80% of time restarting servers. No time to automate. Toil grew worse.

buildup.text: When all time goes to firefighting, none is left to build fire suppression systems.

discovery.text: Google's rule: max 50% toil, minimum 50% engineering that reduces future toil.

twist.text: If toil exceeds 50%, escalate. Add headcount or hand the service back to the dev team.

climax.text: Engineering reduces toil. Less toil frees engineering time. A virtuous cycle starts.

punchline.text: Half ops, half engineering. The 50% rule protects SREs.

quiz.question: What does Google's 50% rule require of SRE teams?
options: At most 50% of time on operational toil, at least 50% on engineering
50% of the team handles ops, 50% writes features
SREs work only 50% of normal hours

hook.text: A new service launched with no monitoring and no runbooks. The first outage was chaos.

buildup.text: A production readiness review checks if a service is ready for SRE support.

discovery.text: The checklist covers monitoring, alerting, SLOs, documentation, and architecture.

twist.text: PRRs aren't gatekeeping. SREs and devs close gaps together before launch, not after.

climax.text: Each postmortem may add a new check. The checklist encodes the team's collective wisdom.

punchline.text: Review before launch. Prevent the first-week outage.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of a Production Readiness Review?
options: To verify a service meets reliability standards before SRE engagement
To delay product launches as long as possible
To evaluate developer performance

hook.text: The on-call got 15 pages in one night. Most were noise. By morning, real alerts were ignored.

buildup.text: Sustainable on-call means manageable load: at most two events per 12-hour shift.

discovery.text: Follow-the-sun rotates on-call across time zones. Nobody takes night shifts.

twist.text: After each shift, review every page. Were they actionable? Fix the noisy ones.

climax.text: Every page should be worth waking up for. If it isn't, delete or tune the alert.

punchline.text: Protect on-call engineers. Every alert must be worth it.

quiz.question: What is a key principle of sustainable on-call?
options: Every alert should be actionable and worth responding to
On-call engineers should handle as many pages as possible
Night shifts build character and should be encouraged

hook.text: Devs ship fast. SREs want stability. The tension can be toxic or become productive balance.

buildup.text: SRE and dev teams need shared goals. Error budgets provide exactly that.

discovery.text: Embed SREs in sprints. They review designs for reliability and suggest monitoring early.

twist.text: Dev teams should share on-call. Feeling production pain improves code quality naturally.

climax.text: Shared ownership removes the blame game. Both teams win when the service is reliable.

punchline.text: Partners, not opponents. Shared goals build collaboration.

quiz.question: How can SRE and development teams collaborate effectively?
options: By sharing goals through error budgets and embedding SREs in sprints
By keeping the teams completely separate
By having developers handle all on-call duties alone

hook.text: SRE started at Google, but LinkedIn, Dropbox, and thousands more adopted it successfully.

buildup.text: You don't need Google's scale. A five-person team can use SLOs and error budgets.

discovery.text: Start with one practice: define SLOs for your top service. See how it changes decisions.

twist.text: Small companies adapt SRE, not copy it. One person can combine SRE with other roles.

climax.text: The SRE Workbook gives practical guidance for teams of any size. Real templates inside.

punchline.text: SRE scales down. Start with one practice. Grow from there.

quiz.question: Can small teams adopt SRE practices?
options: Yes, by starting with one practice like SLOs and adapting to their size
No, SRE only works at Google's scale
Only if they hire a dedicated SRE team of 50+
